title: Mount Hiei

[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Mount+Hiei%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Mount+Hiei%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Mount+Hiei%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Mount+Hiei%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(November 2011)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

(比叡山, *Hiei-zan*) is a mountain to the northeast of [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto"), lying on the border between the [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto_Prefecture "Kyoto Prefecture") and [Shiga Prefectures](/wiki/Shiga_Prefecture "Shiga Prefecture"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan").
(西塔; "West [Pagoda](/wiki/Pagoda "Pagoda")") area near the summit, and technically in [Kyoto Prefecture](/wiki/Kyoto_Prefecture "Kyoto Prefecture").

(東塔; "East [Pagoda](/wiki/Pagoda "Pagoda")") area, also near the summit, where Enryaku-ji Temple was first founded, and located just within [Shiga Prefecture](/wiki/Shiga_Prefecture "Shiga Prefecture").

(横川; "Along the river") area near the northernmost end of Mount Hiei. Due to its remoteness, as a temple complex it experienced periods of revival and decline, starting with [Ennin](/wiki/Ennin "Ennin"), later revived by [Ryōgen](/wiki/Ry%C5%8Dgen "Ryōgen") and made famous by the scholar-monk [Genshin](/wiki/Genshin "Genshin").[[1]](#cite_note-rhodes-1)

Rhodes, Robert F.; Payne, Richard K. (2017). *Genshin's Ōjōyōshū and the Construction of Pure Land Discourse in Heian Japan (Pure Land Buddhist Studies)*. University of Hawaii Press. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0824872489](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0824872489 "Special:BookSources/978-0824872489").

Groner, Paul (2000). *Saicho: The Establishment of the Japanese Tendai School*. University of Hawaii Press. pp. 17–37. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0824823710](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0824823710 "Special:BookSources/0824823710").

Sansom, George (1961). [*A History of Japan 1334–1615*](https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans). Stanford: Stanford University Press. pp. [284](https://archive.org/details/historyofjapan00sans/page/284). [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0804705259](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0804705259 "Special:BookSources/0804705259"). `{{cite book}}`: ISBN / Date incompatibility ([help](/wiki/Help:CS1_errors#invalid_isbn_date "Help:CS1 errors"))
["Monk's Enlightenment Begins With A Marathon Walk"](https://www.npr.org/templates/story/story.php?storyId=125223168). [NPR](/wiki/NPR "NPR"). May 11, 2010. Retrieved October 22, 2015.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Mount_Hiei_Relief_Map%2C_SRTM-1.jpg/250px-Mount_Hiei_Relief_Map%2C_SRTM-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mount_Hiei_Relief_Map,_SRTM-1.jpg)

Relief map of Mount Hiei

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Takaragaike_Park_Kyoto07s3s4350.jpg/250px-Takaragaike_Park_Kyoto07s3s4350.jpg)](/wiki/File:Takaragaike_Park_Kyoto07s3s4350.jpg)

West side

**Mount Hiei** (比叡山, *Hiei-zan*) is a mountain to the northeast of [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto"), lying on the border between the [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto_Prefecture "Kyoto Prefecture") and [Shiga Prefectures](/wiki/Shiga_Prefecture "Shiga Prefecture"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan").

The temple of [Enryaku-ji](/wiki/Enryaku-ji "Enryaku-ji"), the first outpost of the Japanese [Tendai](/wiki/Tendai "Tendai") (Chin. [Tiantai](/wiki/Tiantai "Tiantai")) sect of [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), was founded atop Mount Hiei by [Saichō](/wiki/Saich%C5%8D "Saichō") in 788 and rapidly grew into a sprawling complex of temples and buildings that were roughly divided into three areas:

1. The **Saitō** (西塔; "West [Pagoda](/wiki/Pagoda "Pagoda")") area near the summit, and technically in [Kyoto Prefecture](/wiki/Kyoto_Prefecture "Kyoto Prefecture").
2. The **Tōdō** (東塔; "East [Pagoda](/wiki/Pagoda "Pagoda")") area, also near the summit, where Enryaku-ji Temple was first founded, and located just within [Shiga Prefecture](/wiki/Shiga_Prefecture "Shiga Prefecture").
3. The **Yokawa** (横川; "Along the river") area near the northernmost end of Mount Hiei. Due to its remoteness, as a temple complex it experienced periods of revival and decline, starting with [Ennin](/wiki/Ennin "Ennin"), later revived by [Ryōgen](/wiki/Ry%C5%8Dgen "Ryōgen") and made famous by the scholar-monk [Genshin](/wiki/Genshin "Genshin").[[1]](#cite_note-rhodes-1)

Due to its position north-east of the ancient capital of [Heian-kyō](/wiki/Heian-ky%C5%8D "Heian-kyō") ([Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto")), it was thought in ancient [geomancy](/wiki/Feng_shui "Feng shui") to be a protective bulwark against negative influences on the capital,[[2]](#cite_note-groner-book-2) which along with the rise of the Tendai sect in the [Heian period](/wiki/Heian_period "Heian period") (8th - 12th centuries) meant that the mountain and the temple complex were politically powerful and influential. Later schools of Buddhism in Japan were almost entirely founded by ex-monks of the Tendai sect, such as [Hōnen](/wiki/H%C5%8Dnen "Hōnen"), [Nichiren](/wiki/Nichiren "Nichiren"), [Dōgen](/wiki/D%C5%8Dgen "Dōgen") and [Shinran](/wiki/Shinran "Shinran"), who all studied at the temple before leaving Mount Hiei to start their own practices.

The temple complex [was razed by Oda Nobunaga](/wiki/Siege_of_Mount_Hiei "Siege of Mount Hiei") in 1571 to quell the rising power of Tendai's [sōhei](/wiki/S%C5%8Dhei "Sōhei") (warrior monks).[[3]](#cite_note-Stanford-3) It was rebuilt and remains Tendai headquarters to this day.

The 19th-century [Japanese ironclad *Hiei*](/wiki/Japanese_ironclad_Hiei "Japanese ironclad Hiei") was named after this mountain, as was the more famous [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II")-era battleship [*Hiei*](/wiki/Japanese_battleship_Hiei "Japanese battleship Hiei"), the latter having initially been built as a battlecruiser.

## Mount Hiei in folklore

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mount_Hiei&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Mount Hiei in folklore")]

Mount Hiei has been featured in many folk tales over the ages. Initially, it was thought to be the home of indigenous gods and spirits, and there was a [shrine](/wiki/Shinto_shrine "Shinto shrine") to [Oyamakui no Kami](/wiki/Oyamakui_no_Kami "Oyamakui no Kami"), also known in the [shinbutsu-shūgō](/wiki/Shinbutsu-sh%C5%ABg%C5%8D "Shinbutsu-shūgō") system as Sannō Gongen (山王権現). However, it is now predominantly known for the monks of Enryaku-ji.

## Marathon monks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mount_Hiei&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Marathon monks")]

[John Stevens](/wiki/John_Stevens_(scholar) "John Stevens (scholar)") wrote the book *The Marathon Monks of Mount Hiei,* chronicling the practice of walking long distances – up to 52 miles (84 km) a day for 100 straight days, in an effort to attain enlightenment. The practice of walking is known as the *[kaihōgyō](/wiki/Kaih%C5%8Dgy%C5%8D "Kaihōgyō")*.

A 2010 US [National Public Radio](/wiki/NPR "NPR") report described the *sennichi kaihōgyō* (thousand-day *kaihōgyō*) as

> ...1,000 days of walking meditation and prayer over a seven-year period around Mount Hiei. [The 13th disciple since WWII to complete the cycle] walked 26 miles a day for periods of either 100 or 200 consecutive days — a total distance about the same as walking around the Earth.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Attractions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mount_Hiei&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Attractions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Enryakuji_Konponchudo02s5s3200.jpg/250px-Enryakuji_Konponchudo02s5s3200.jpg)](/wiki/File:Enryakuji_Konponchudo02s5s3200.jpg)

Famous temple [Enryaku-ji](/wiki/Enryaku-ji "Enryaku-ji")

Beyond the mountain itself, its forests, and the views it affords – of Kyoto, of Ohara, of lake Biwa and Shiga – the main attraction is the temple complex of [Enryaku-ji](/wiki/Enryaku-ji "Enryaku-ji"). The temple complex spreads out over the mountain, but is concentrated in three areas, connected by foot trails. There are also more minor temples and shrines.

Unusually, there are also a number of French-themed attractions – the peak itself features the Garden Museum Hiei, which is themed on French impressionism, featuring gardens and French paintings, while there is also a French-themed hotel, "L'hotel de Hiei" (The Hiei Hotel). The mountain is busiest during the daytime, but has some visitors in the evenings, for light-up displays and to see the night view of the surrounding towns.

## Access

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mount_Hiei&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Access")]

The mountain is a popular area for [hikers](/wiki/Hiking "Hiking") and a toll road provides access by automobile to the top of the mountain; there are also buses that connect the mountaintop to town a few times a day. There are also two routes of [funiculars](/wiki/Funicular "Funicular"): the [Eizan Cable](/wiki/Keifuku_Cable_Line "Keifuku Cable Line") from the Kyoto side to the connecting point with an [aerial tramway](/wiki/Aerial_tramway "Aerial tramway") ("ropeway") to the top, and the [Sakamoto Cable](/wiki/Sakamoto_Cable "Sakamoto Cable") from the Shiga side to the foot of Enryaku-ji.

The attractions on the mountain are quite spread out, so there are regular buses during the daytime connecting the attractions. The center for these is the bus center, in front of the entrance to the main temple complex at *Tō-tō* (東塔; "East Pagoda").

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mount_Hiei&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: See also")]

* [Kaihōgyō](/wiki/Kaih%C5%8Dgy%C5%8D "Kaihōgyō")
